Backtesting using vectorbt — cookbook (Part 3)

Tobi Lux
14 min read
·
Feb 5, 2026

This is part-3 of a cookbook for using essential elements of vectorbt (vbt) for backtesting. Note: my statements are valid for the free version of vbt only (version 0.28.1).

As always, the full code is available on my GitHub — see also the disclaimer at the end.

Content of the blog

This series of blogs has the following content:

  • Part 1: Setting the scene, getting started with vbt, Overview on methods and parameters (for calculation of metrics, plotting, logging etc.)
  • Part 2: trade-prices (default/modified), impact of parameters as size_type, size, min_size, max_size, fees, slippage, standard StopLoss and TakeProfit-behaviour
  • Part 3: (this part) callback-functions in vbt, using callbacks for customizing Stops
  • Part 4 : multiple orders per position, impact of accumulation, accept_partial, StopLoss and TakeProfit for multiple orders, ShortOnly trades, LongShort trades
  • Part 5 : multi-ticker portfolio and standard outputs, cash distributions, cash-pooling (with and without order size limitations), groups of tickers, group-cash-pools (with and without order size-limitations), LongShortStrategies in multiticker-portfolios
  • Part 6: some use-cases as batch-testing of different strategies, optimisation of parameters of trading strategies, sector rotation, etc…

1. Data Download and Signal Generation

In this section, we:

  • Pull price data directly from yfinance
  • Compute technical indicators using the ta library
  • Build a simple SMA-crossover, long-only strategy for a single-ticker portfolio

See the previous parts for more explanation.

# download data
ticker = 'AAPL'
start_date = '2022-01-01'
end_date = '2025-10-11'
prices = yf.download(ticker, start=start_date, end=end_date, auto_adjust=True)

# preparing for ta & vbt-use (remove MultiLevel-column-index, transform to a pd.Series
close = prices.Close[ticker]
open = prices.Open[ticker]
high = prices.High[ticker]
low = prices.Low[ticker]

# entry and exit signals via ta,
fast_window = 10
slow_window = 60

fast_sma = ta.trend.sma_indicator(close, window=fast_window)
slow_sma = ta.trend.sma_indicator(close, window=slow_window)

# Entry and Exit Signals by simple SMA-crossing
entries = ((fast_sma > slow_sma) & (fast_sma.shift(1) < slow_sma.shift(1))).rename(ticker)
exits = ((fast_sma < slow_sma) & (fast_sma.shift(1) > slow_sma.shift(1))).rename(ticker)

2. Working With Callback Functions in vectorbt

Callback functions in vbt let you customize certain calculations during backtesting. Covering this advanced topic in full is beyond the scope of this article, so we’ll focus on one practical use case: customizing exits with TakeProfit and StopLoss rules.

Callback functions run on every bar, processing (and modifying) attributes. To preserve vbt’s high-performance advantages, these functions must be Numba-compatible.

During each bar’s execution, vbt receives/calculates/provides attributes through the context object (c), which the callback function can access directly. These data are available for further processing.

2.1 Context Object (c)

The context object (c) is an instance of AdjustSLContext. It is an immutable named tuple containing all per-bar attributes passed into the callback function. The attributes fall into four categories: location, stop-related, price, and trade attributes.

Location Attributes

  • c.i — Current row index
  • c.col — Current column index

Stop-Related Attributes

  • c.curr_stop — The sl_loss value provided when the portfolio was created via vbt.Portfolio.from_order(...), represents the stop-loss percentage, if in a position, NaN otherwise.
  • c.curr_trail — The sl_trail flag set during portfolio creation; True if trailing stops are enabled, False otherwise.

Price Attributes

  • c.val_price_now — Current price used for validation (default: close).

Trade Attributes

  • c.init_i — Bar index where the current trade started, if in position; -1 otherwise.
  • c.position_now — Number of shares currently held, if in position, NaN otherwise.
  • c.curr_price — Trailing reference price for the active trade.
  • With OHLC data: curr_price[i] = max(high[i-1], curr_price[i-1])
  • With close-only data: curr_price[i] = max(close[i-1], curr_price[i-1])

Note: c.curr_price is solely a trailing reference price. To compute the actual StopLoss price at bar [i], we need to use:
stop_loss_price [i] = c.curr_price[i] × (1 − c.curr_stop)

2. The context object includes no dedicated TakeProfit fields.
This is by design, since the TakeProfit-price can be derived directly:
take_profit_price = c.curr_price[i] × (1 + tp_stop)

A trailing TakeProfit is not supported in vbt.

2.2 Logging the Content of the Context Object (c)

Before customizing stop logic, we first need to understand what data the context object actually contains.

To inspect it, we assign a callback function — here called adjust_sl_func_nb_01—to the adjust_sl_func_nb parameter of the .from_signals(...) method. Its sole purpose is to record the contents of the context object for later analysis.

Because callback functions must follow strict Numba-compatibility rules, there are important constraints:

  • A callback must return exactly two values: c.curr_stop and c.curr_trail. This means we cannot return the context object itself.
  • To log data, we therefore create a global logger object (e.g., a Python list or NumPy array). We pass a reference to this logger into the callback, and the callback mutates it in place.
  • Since the logger is global, we can access and analyze its contents after the portfolio generation step.
  • The context object c must always be included in the callback's parameter list.

With these requirements in mind, the logging-only callback function has the following structure:

@njit
def adjust_sl_func_nb_01(c, logger: np.ndarray) -> tuple[float, float]:
    ''' returns available paramters of context object (c)
    Args:
        c: vectorbt context object
        logger (np.ndarray): numpy array to log the context object attributes
    Returns:
        tuple[float, float]: current stop loss and current trail
    '''
    i = c.i
    logger[i, 0] = c.val_price_now
    logger[i, 1] = c.init_price
    logger[i, 2] = c.curr_stop
    logger[i, 3] = c.curr_trail
    logger[i, 4] = c.i
    logger[i, 5] = c.init_i
    logger[i, 6] = c.col
    logger[i, 7] = c.position_now
    logger[i, 8] = c.curr_price

    return c.curr_stop, c.curr_trail

In the next code block, we first initialize the logger.

We then create a portfolio instance that uses a 5% trailing StopLoss and no TakeProfit, while simultaneously logging the content of the context object (c).

To enable this, we must pass:

  • to adjust_sl_func_nb → the callback function adjust_sl_func_nb_01
  • to adjust_sl_args → all arguments required by adjust_sl_func_nb_01 except the context object c

Important: adjust_sl_args must always be a tuple. Therefore, the logger must be passed as (logger_sl,) — not (logger_sl).

# initializing logger
shape = close.shape
logger_sl = np.zeros((shape[0], 9))

# Paramters for Take Profit and Stop Loss
stop_loss = 0.05            # % 5%
stop_loss_trail = True      # trailing-stop-loss
take_profit = np.inf        # no take profit

# generate a portfolio instance with trailing stop loss
pf = vbt.Portfolio.from_signals(
    close=close,
    entries=entries,
    exits=exits,
    sl_stop=stop_loss,
    sl_trail=stop_loss_trail,
    adjust_sl_func_nb=adjust_sl_func_nb_01,
    adjust_sl_args=(logger_sl,),
    tp_stop=take_profit,
    freq='D',
    init_cash=10_000,
    high=high,
    low=low,
)

Next, we extract the trades (including exit classifications; see Part 2 for details). After that, we build the timeline (also discussed in Part 2).

To the standard timeline content — OHLC data, entry/exit signals, and effective entries/exits — we now append the data recorded by our logger.

Finally, we add the current Stop-Loss price, i.e., the actual price level that would trigger the stop in practice.

# list of trades with key data, amended by the trade type
trades = pf.trades.records_readable.copy()
trades['exit_type'] = trades.apply(get_exit_type, axis=1, args=(take_profit, exits))

# generating timeline for standard + specific trading parameters
timeline = get_timeline_extended(prices, entries, exits, trades)    # standards (prices, entry/exit-signals, effective entries and exits)
df_log_sl = pd.DataFrame(logger_sl, index=close.index,              # specifics: adding the logger
                         columns=['val_price_now', 'init_price', 'cur_stop', 'cur_trail', 'i', 'init_i', 'col', 'position_now', 'curr_price'])
timeline = pd.concat([timeline, df_log_sl], axis=1)
timeline['sl_calc'] = timeline['curr_price'] * (1 - stop_loss)      # adding calculated sl

trades is as in Part 2. Our dataframe timeline contains the following columns:

Index(['open', 'high', 'low', 'close', 'sys_entries', 'sys_exits',
       'eff_entries', 'eff_exits_system', 'eff_exits_stop_loss',
       'eff_exits_take_profit', 'val_price_now', 'init_price', 'cur_stop',
       'cur_trail', 'i', 'init_i', 'col', 'position_now', 'curr_price',
       'sl_calc'],
      dtype='object')

Note: Once again, we avoid excessive use of screenshots and focus instead on describing the behavior of the Portfolio—supported by the logged values included in the timeline. For full details and verification, please refer to the notebooks on my GitHub.

2.3 Stop-Loss Trigger Details

In Part 2, we could not directly illustrate the mechanics of triggering a Stop-Loss in detail. With the logger data now available, we can examine this behavior.

Looking at the timeline around the first entry signal in our Portfolio instance, we observe:

  • Entry signal at bar [i]
  • init_price: [i] = NaN, [i+1] = close[i]
  • cur_stop: [i] = NaN, [i+1] = 0.05 (=sl_stop)
  • cur_trail: [i] = 0.0, [i+1] = 1.0 (corresponds to sl_trail = True)
  • init_i: [i] = -1.0, [i+1] = i
  • position_now: [i] = 0.0, [i+1] = 67.43 (number of assets)
  • cur_price: [i] = NaN, [i+1] = close[i]
  • sl_calc: [i] = NaN, [i+1] = cur_price × (1 − sl_stop)
    (Note: sl_calc is computed separately and was not logged in c.)

When inspecting the timeline during in-position bars, e.g., bar [k], we observe:

  • init_price[k] remains unchanged
  • cur_stop[k] and cur_trail[k] remain fixed at the values of sl_stop and sl_trail
  • init_i[k] remains at the original entry index i
  • position_now[k] remains constant
  • cur_price[k] = max(high[k-1], cur_price[k-1])
  • sl_calc[k] = cur_price[k] × (1 − sl_stop)

Important: Except for the first bar of a trade, the trailing price (cur_price[k]) is calculated using only the maximum of the previous highs and the previous cur_price, i.e., data up to the previous bar.

  • Multiplying cur_price[k] by (1 + tp_stop) gives the actual Take-Profit level.
  • Multiplying by (1 − sl_stop) gives the actual trailing Stop-Loss level.

As a result, the TakeProfit and StopLoss prices are based on the highs of the asset prices and are slightly more conservative (higher-level = later trigger for TakeProfit, or earlier trigger for StopLoss) compared to stops calculated using the close price alone.

Get Tobi Lux’s stories in your inbox

Join Medium for free to get updates from this writer.

Now, let’s examine how an exit signal is triggered with a trailing StopLoss (at bar [j]):

  • All parameters are updated according to the rules described for in-position bars.
  • Bar [j] is the first bar where: low[j] < cur_price[j] × (1 − sl_stop)

Note: StopLoss signals are triggered using the lows of the asset prices, if low is provided as a parameter to Portfolio (as in our current case). Otherwise, StopLoss signals are calculated using the default method, based on the close prices.

After the exit signal at bar [j], we observe the following at bar [j+1]:

  • init_price[j+1] = NaN
  • cur_stop[j+1] = NaN, cur_trail[j+1] = 0.0
  • init_i[j+1] = -1.0
  • position_now[j+1] = 0.0
  • cur_price[j+1] = NaN
  • sl_calc[j+1] = NaN

From the next bar onward, all parameters are reset and ready for the next entry signal.

3. Extracting TakeProfit and StopLoss Prices Using Callbacks

In addition to adjust_sl_func_nb, which lets you specify a callback for StopLosses, vbt provides adjust_tp_func_nb for TakeProfit customization. In the following example, we use these callbacks to return:

  • The price of a trailing StopLoss
  • The entry price of a position
  • The price of a TakeProfit (limit)

This enables us to plot these levels on a chart for better visualization.

First, we define the two required callback functions:

@njit
def adjust_sl_func_nb_02(c, sl_price: np.ndarray, init_price: np.array) -> tuple[float, float]:
    ''' Records current SL stop and initial_price
    Args:
        c: AdjustSLContext (context for current row/column)
        sl_price: array for logging the Stop-Loss-prices
    Returns:
        tuple[float, float]: current stop loss and current trail
    '''
    i = c.i
    sl_price[i] = c.curr_price * (1 - stop_loss)
    init_price[i] = c.init_price

    return c.curr_stop, c.curr_trail
@njit
def adjust_tp_func_nb_02(c, tp_stop, tp_price: np.ndarray) -> float:
    ''' Records current TP stop,
    Args:
        c: AdjustTPContext (context for current row/column)
        tp_stop: current value for taking profit limits (%)
        tp_price: array for logging Take-Profit-Prices
    Returns:
        float: the current TP stop (scalar)
    '''
    i = c.i
    if c.init_i == -1:
        tp_price[i] = np.nan
    else:
        tp_price[i] = c.init_price * (1 + tp_stop)

    return c.curr_stop

We initialize our loggers (for the parameters of interest), define the TakeProfit and StopLoss-parameters and generate an instance of the portfolio-class as follows:

# initializing logger
shape = close.shape
sl_price = np.zeros(shape[0])
in_price = np.zeros(shape[0])
tp_price = np.zeros(shape[0])

# Paramters for Take Profit and Stop Loss
stop_loss = 0.05            # 5%
stop_loss_trail = True      # trailing-stop-loss
take_profit = 0.15          # 15%

# generate a portfolio instance with trailing stop loss and take profit
pf = vbt.Portfolio.from_signals(
    close=close,
    entries=entries,
    exits=exits,
    sl_stop=stop_loss,
    sl_trail=stop_loss_trail,
    adjust_sl_func_nb=adjust_sl_func_nb_02,
    adjust_sl_args=(sl_price, in_price),
    tp_stop=take_profit,
    adjust_tp_func_nb=adjust_tp_func_nb_02,
    adjust_tp_args=(take_profit, tp_price),
    freq='D',
    init_cash=10_000,
    high=high,
    low=low,
)

We then tabulate the trades (including exit classifications) and generate a custom timeline containing the data of interest.

This timeline now includes the true Stop-Loss levels, entry prices, and Take-Profit limits, enabling detailed analysis and visualization.

# list of trades with key data, amended by the trade type
trades = pf.trades.records_readable.copy()
trades['exit_type'] = trades.apply(get_exit_type, axis=1, args=(take_profit, exits))

# generating timeline for standard + specific trading parameters
timeline = get_timeline_extended(prices, entries, exits, trades)    # standards (prices, entry/exit-signals, effective entries and exits)

timeline['fast_sma'] = fast_sma
timeline['slow_sma'] = slow_sma
timeline['sl_price'] = sl_price  # adding our logged parameters
timeline['in_price'] = in_price
timeline['tp_price'] = tp_price

timeline['position'] = np.sign(pf.net_exposure())

We again use our flexible plotting function, plot_multi_subplot_trading_data, to create an overview chart that includes:

  • Candlestick chart with fast and slow SMAs
  • All entry points
  • All exits, separated by type
  • StopLoss and TakeProfit prices (while in position)
  • Relevant entry price (while in position)
  • A subplot showing the current position

For a clearer visual demonstration, we do not plot the full timeline. Instead, we slice a typical section and plot only this segment.

# focusing on an intervall for analyses
start_analyses='2023-10-01'
end_analyses='2024-06-30'
timeline_slice = timeline.loc[start_analyses:end_analyses]

fig = plot_multi_subplot_trading_data(
    timeline_slice,
    subplot_config_02,
    title = f'slice for {ticker}, from {start_analyses} to {end_analyses}',
    size=(1400, 500),
    theme='plotly',
)
fig.show()

We can now observe the indicators and their crossings, the trailing behavior of the Stop-Loss, the fixed Take-Profit levels, and the consistency between positions and effective entry and exit signals.

Note: In the figure, red triangles indicating strategy-triggered exits are not visible. This is due to our tight TakeProfit and StopLoss settings, which enforce very early exits. With different parameter choices, strategy-triggered exits would also be observable.

4. Customized Stops

4.1 Example 1: StopLoss Based on Trade Profit

Now, let’s apply our knowledge to customize StopLoss behavior.

We define the following StopLoss logic:

  • Trade profit ≤ 10%: sl_stop = 10%, sl_trail = False
  • 10% < Trade profit ≤ 20%: tighten stop to sl_stop = 5%, keep sl_trail = False
  • Trade profit > 20%: keep sl_stop = 5%, but enable sl_trail = True

This system tightens the StopLoss as the trade becomes more profitable.

We implement this logic using the following callback function:

@njit
def adjust_sl_func_nb_03(c, sl_price: np.ndarray, init_price: np.ndarray, trade_profit: np.ndarray) -> tuple[float, float]:
    ''' Adjust stop-loss price and trailing behavior dynamically based on current trade profit
        Note: this version only covers LongOnly-Strategies
    Args:
        c:            AdjustSLContext (context for current row/column)
        sl_price:     array to record the stop-loss price
        init_price:   array to record the initial entry price
        trade_profit: array to record the trade-profit
    Returns:
        tuple[float, float]: (current stop loss fraction, trailing flag)
    '''
    # Current trade profit (fraction, e.g., 0.12 = +12%)

    i = c.i

    ctp = np.nan
    if not np.isnan(c.init_price):          # in active trade
        ctp = (c.val_price_now - c.init_price) / c.init_price

    # Determine SL and trailing behavior, depending o current trailing behaviour
    if ctp < 0.10:
        sl_stop = 0.10
        sl_trail = False
    elif ctp <= 0.20:
        sl_stop = 0.05
        sl_trail = False
    else:  # ctp > 0.20
        sl_stop = 0.05
        sl_trail = True

    # Record SL and initial prices for later analysis/plotting
    sl_price[i] = max(c.curr_price * (1 - sl_stop), sl_price[i-1])
    init_price[i] = c.init_price
    trade_profit[i] = ctp

    return sl_stop, sl_trail

We generate a Portfolio instance:

# initializing logger
shape = close.shape
sl_price = np.zeros(shape[0])
in_price = np.zeros(shape[0])
tp_price = np.zeros(shape[0])
trade_profit = np.zeros(shape[0])

# No Paramters for Stop Loss (controlled by callback), no take_profit
take_profit = np.inf

# generate a portfolio instance with customized (trailing)-stop
pf = vbt.Portfolio.from_signals(
    close=close,
    entries=entries,
    exits=exits,
    adjust_sl_func_nb=adjust_sl_func_nb_03,
    adjust_sl_args=(sl_price, in_price, trade_profit),
    freq='D',
    init_cash=10_000,
    high=high,
    low=low,
)

Now we generate the trades list and the timelines with the relevant data. When slicing the timeline to a certain interval and using our plotting function plot_multi_subplot_trading_data, we get the following plot:

We focus on the third trade as an example (the rightmost trade on the plot).

  • Entry: Bar [i] (2023-01-30). With sl_stop = 0.1, the initial Stop-Loss price is set to 90% of init_price (close[i] × (1 − sl_stop)). Since sl_trail = False, it remains fixed.
  • Bar [j] (2023-03-21): Trade profit exceeds the first threshold (>10%). The callback updates sl_stop = 5% while keeping sl_trail = False. The Stop-Loss price is lifted by 5%, producing a small upward step.
  • Bar [k] (2023-05-05): Trade profit exceeds 20%, triggering sl_trail = True (with sl_stop = 5%). The Stop-Loss behavior switches to trailing mode, and the price is (significantly) readjusted to high[k] × (1 − sl_stop), resulting in a larger upward jump.
  • Exit: Bar [m] (2023-08-04), when the position is closed because low[m] falls below the trailing Stop-Loss price.

4.2 Example 2: Trailing Stop-Loss Based on ATR at Entry

The Average True Range (ATR) measures price volatility. To avoid premature exits during periods of high volatility, we set the initial Stop-Loss lower when volatility is high and tighter when volatility is low.

At entry (bar [i]), the Stop-Loss is calculated as:

  • stop_loss[i] = close[i] - factor × ATR[i]or equivalently:
  • stop_loss[i] = close[i] × (1 − (factor × ATR[i]) / close[i])

In this strategy, the value of (factor × ATR) is fixed for the trade based on the ATR at the time of entry.

The callback function implementing this logic is as follows:

@njit
def adjust_sl_func_nb_04(c, sl_price: np.ndarray, init_price: np.ndarray, atr: np.ndarray, factor: float) -> tuple[float, bool]:
    ''' inital Stop-Loss set a init_price - factor * ATR, then trailing
        LongOnly Strategy
    Args:
        c:          AdjustSLContext
        sl_price:   array to record stop-loss price (for plotting)
        init_price: array to record entry price
        atr:        precomputed ATR array
        factor:     ATR multiplier (float)
    Returns:
        (sl_stop, sl_trail)
    '''
    i = c.i
    # New trade entry detected
    if not np.isnan(c.init_price) and (i == 0 or np.isnan(init_price[i - 1])):
        # compute fractional stop distance so absolute stop = init_price - factor*ATR
        sl_stop = (factor * atr[i-1]) / c.init_price   # use bar, where signal was generated
        sl_trail = True
        sl_price[i] = c.init_price - factor * atr[i-1]

    # Ongoing trade: do NOT recompute sl_stop from ATR — keep current
    elif not np.isnan(c.init_price):
        sl_stop = c.curr_stop
        sl_trail = True
        sl_price[i] = max(c.curr_price * (1 - sl_stop), sl_price[i-1])

    # No active trade
    else:
        sl_stop = np.nan
        sl_trail = False
        sl_price[i] = np.nan

    # record entry price (NaN when no trade)
    init_price[i] = c.init_price

    return sl_stop, sl_trail

After initializing the loggers, setting the Stop-Loss and Take-Profit parameters, defining the factor, and calculating the ATR (using the function provided by ta), we generate a portfolio instance as follows:

# initializing logger
shape = close.shape
sl_price = np.zeros(shape[0])
in_price = np.zeros(shape[0])

# No Paramters for Stop Loss (controlled by callback), no take_profit
take_profit = np.inf

# ATR-stop-parameter
factor = 3.0
window = 14
atr = ta.volatility.AverageTrueRange(high=high,low=low,close=close,
                                     window=window,
                                     ).average_true_range().to_numpy()

# generate a portfolio instance with customized (trailing)-stop
pf = vbt.Portfolio.from_signals(
    close=close,
    entries=entries,
    exits=exits,
    adjust_sl_func_nb=adjust_sl_func_nb_04,
    adjust_sl_args=(sl_price, in_price, atr, factor),
    freq='D',
    init_cash=10_000,
    high=high,
    low=low,
)

We generate the trade-table and the timelines (this time including the ATR). When slicing it an using our function plot_multi_subplot_trading_data, we get the following plot:

In the middle subplot, we see the ATR timeline.

It is evident that the ATR at the entry of the first trade is higher than at the entry of the second trade. As a result, the distance between the initial Stop-Loss level and the entry price is larger for the first trade than for the second.

Both trades are eventually stopped out by the trailing Stop-Loss.

NOTE: The later example is for demonstration purposes only. The same behaviour can be (much simpler and more directly) achieved by handing over the series ATR * factor / close to the stop_loss parameter.

Conclusion

In this part, we took a deep dive into callback functions in vbt. We explored the context object (c) by logging its content and learned how to extract parameters to calculate key metrics, such as timelines of TakeProfit and StopLoss prices.

Using this knowledge, we were able to plot these parameters and to develop customized exit strategies by applying callback functions for dynamic trailing Stop-Losses.

This foundation will serve as the basis for the next parts. Stay tuned!
